column1: #deck:k8s-platform-labs::02-deployments::02-rolling-update-recreate
#separator:Comma
#columns:Front
column2: Back

column1: When a rolling update happens, does the existing ReplicaSet get mutated in place?
column2: No — a new ReplicaSet is created with a new pod-template-hash; the old one scales down to 0 rather than being changed

column1: What does maxSurge control?
column2: How many extra pods beyond the desired replica count can exist during a rollout

column1: What does maxUnavailable: 0 actually guarantee?
column2: Zero downtime, but only if new pods actually pass readiness — without a real readiness probe, Ready happens the instant the container starts

column1: Why might a rollout with maxSurge:1 stall indefinitely?
column2: The surge pod is scheduled like any other pod — if its resource requests can't be satisfied by any node, it never schedules and the rollout never progresses

column1: Does kubectl rollout undo restore the old revision number, or create a new one?
column2: It creates a new revision copying the old configuration forward — the old revision number disappears from history

column1: What happens if you kubectl rollout undo --to-revision=N and N doesn't exist?
column2: kubectl fails outright: 'error: unable to find specified revision N in history'

column1: What bounds how far back you can roll back a Deployment?
column2: spec.revisionHistoryLimit (default 10) — revisions beyond that are pruned and can no longer be targeted

column1: Is the --record flag still safe to rely on for tracking change-cause?
column2: No — officially deprecated per the Kubernetes documentation, and current kubectl versions may already reject it outright as an unrecognized flag. Use the kubernetes.io/change-cause annotation directly instead

column1: What does kubectl rollout pause actually freeze?
column2: The Deployment controller stops acting on further template changes mid-rollout, leaving a mix of old and new pods until resumed

column1: What's the practical difference between RollingUpdate and Recreate strategies?
column2: RollingUpdate replaces pods gradually with no downtime (if configured well); Recreate kills all old pods first, then creates new ones, causing a downtime window

column1: Does kubectl rollout restart only work under the RollingUpdate strategy, and does it leave the pod template unchanged?
column2: No to both — it follows whichever strategy is currently configured (RollingUpdate or Recreate), and it does change the template by adding a restartedAt annotation, which is exactly what triggers the rollout

column1: If you apply a second Deployment update while the first rollout is still in progress, does Kubernetes wait?
column2: No — this is Rollover. A new ReplicaSet is created immediately from the new template, while the previously-newest ReplicaSet is rolled into the old list and scaled down alongside the rest

column1: If you scale a Deployment mid-rollout, do all new replicas go to the newest ReplicaSet?
column2: No — proportional scaling distributes the additional replicas across every ReplicaSet still in play, weighted by current share

column1: Can maxSurge and maxUnavailable both be set to 0?
column2: No — rejected outright by the API, since that combination would leave a rollout with no way to ever make progress

column1: Does ProgressDeadlineExceeded trigger an automatic rollback?
column2: No — it's purely a reported condition on the Deployment; the stalled ReplicaSet stays exactly as stuck as it was until you intervene manually

column1: What does spec.strategy.type: Recreate actually guarantee?
column2: Old pods are all terminated before any new pod is created — no overlap between old and new, ever

column1: Does spec.strategy.rollingUpdate have any effect when type is Recreate?
column2: No — it's accepted by the schema but silently ignored; maxSurge/maxUnavailable are RollingUpdate-only fields

column1: Under Recreate, can two ReplicaSets ever both have nonzero replicas at once?
column2: No — the old ReplicaSet always reaches 0 before the new one starts scaling up, unlike RollingUpdate where both briefly coexist

column1: A bad image is deployed under Recreate. What happens to availability?
column2: Full outage — the old ReplicaSet has already scaled to 0 before the new, broken ReplicaSet fails to become Ready, leaving no fallback pod

column1: Does kubectl rollout undo behave differently depending on the current strategy?
column2: The mechanism (revision history, undo target) is identical either way — but a Recreate rollback still goes through a full terminate-then-create cycle, unlike a RollingUpdate rollback

column1: When is Recreate actually the right strategy choice?
column2: When two versions genuinely can't coexist (e.g. a breaking DB migration), or in dev/test environments where downtime is a non-issue and configuration simplicity matters more

column1: How does a bad-image rollout differ in outcome between RollingUpdate (maxUnavailable:0) and Recreate?
column2: RollingUpdate stays fully available since old pods are never removed until a replacement is Ready; Recreate causes a full outage since the old ReplicaSet is already gone
